html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夷堅支志 - Title of a specific section or branch of the 'Yijian Zhi' (Record of Strange Events), a collection of strange tales and anecdotes compiled by Hong Mai during the Song Dynasty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宋 - Refers to the Song Dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history known for its cultural and literary achievements.
  • 洪迈 - A prominent scholar and official during the Southern Song Dynasty, known for his contributions to literature and his role as a government official.
  • The 'Yijian Zhi' is a well-known collection of stories and anecdotes from the Song Dynasty, often used to illustrate moral and ethical lessons.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夷坚志 - Title of the book, meaning 'The Record of the Immortal Rock,' a collection of strange tales and supernatural stories.
  • 番阳洪公迈 - Refers to Hong Mai, a prominent scholar and official from Fanyang (modern-day Jiujiang, Jiangxi). '公' is a respectful title for an official or elder.
  • 游宦四方 - Means 'to travel and serve in various regions,' indicating that Hong Mai held positions in different parts of China.
  • 采摭众事 - Means 'to gather and collect various events,' referring to the process of compiling stories and anecdotes.
  • 镂板 - Refers to the process of carving woodblocks for printing books. '不一' means 'not uniform,' suggesting that there were multiple editions.
  • 蜀本 - Refers to the edition printed in Sichuan.
  • 婺本 - Refers to the edition printed in Wuchang (modern-day Jiujiang, Jiangxi).
  • 闽本 - Refers to the edition printed in Fujian.
  • 古杭 - Refers to ancient Hangzhou, a major cultural and economic center.
  • 锓梓 - Another term for carving woodblocks for printing.
  • 遗缺甚多 - Means 'many missing parts,' indicating that the existing copies are incomplete.
  • 张府判绍先 - Refers to Zhang Shaoxian, a local official who oversaw the school affairs.
  • 友人周宏翁 - Refers to a friend named Zhou Hong, who still had a copy of the book.
  • 分甲乙至壬癸为十志 - Means 'divided into ten sections from Jia to Gui,' referring to the ten sections of the book.
  • 详略不同 - Means 'detailed and brief differently,' indicating that the different editions vary in their level of detail.
  • 勉予访寻旧本补之 - Means 'urged me to search for old copies to fill in the gaps.'
  • 闽板久缺 - Means 'the Fujian edition is long missing,' indicating that the Fujian edition is difficult to find.
  • 沈天佑序 - The author of this preface is Shen Tianyou, who wrote this introduction.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 夷坚志 is a significant work in Chinese literature, compiled by Hong Mai, containing a vast array of supernatural and strange tales.
  • 番阳洪公迈 was a renowned scholar and official during the Southern Song Dynasty, known for his contributions to literature and history.
  • 游宦四方 reflects the common practice of officials moving between different regions to serve in various capacities.
  • 采摭众事 was a common practice in ancient Chinese literature, involving the collection and compilation of stories.
  • 镂板 was a major technological advancement in ancient China, allowing for the mass production of books.
  • 蜀本, 婺本, 闽本 refer to different editions of the same book, each printed in a different region, reflecting the widespread distribution and popularity of the work.
  • 古杭 was a major cultural and economic center during the Song Dynasty, known for its advanced printing industry.
  • 锓梓 is another term for woodblock printing, emphasizing the technical process involved.
  • 遗缺甚多 indicates that the existing copies of the book are incomplete, highlighting the importance of preserving and restoring the text.
  • 张府判绍先 was a local official who played a role in the preservation and restoration of the book.
  • 友人周宏翁 was a friend who still had a copy of the book, which was crucial for the restoration efforts.
  • 分甲乙至壬癸为十志 indicates that the book is divided into ten sections, each named after a Chinese character from Jia to Gui, reflecting the systematic organization of the content.
  • 详略不同 indicates that the different editions of the book vary in their level of detail, which can affect the completeness and accuracy of the text.
  • 勉予访寻旧本补之 reflects the collaborative effort to restore the book, with the author being urged to search for old copies to fill in the gaps.
  • 闽板久缺 emphasizes the challenges in the restoration process, as the Fujian edition is particularly difficult to find.
  • 沈天佑序 provides important context and background information about the book and its restoration.

Proper Nouns

  • 夷坚志 - The title of the book.
  • 番阳洪公迈 - Hong Mai, the compiler of the book.
  • 蜀本 - The Sichuan edition of the book.
  • 婺本 - The Wuchang (Jiujiang, Jiangxi) edition of the book.
  • 闽本 - The Fujian edition of the book.
  • 古杭 - Ancient Hangzhou.
  • 张府判绍先 - Zhang Shaoxian, a local official.
  • 友人周宏翁 - Zhou Hong, a friend of the author.
  • 沈天佑序 - The preface written by Shen Tianyou.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夷堅志 - Title of a collection of stories, often translated as 'The Records of the Unusual' or 'The Collection of the Unusual.' It is a well-known work in Chinese literature, particularly for its collection of strange tales and legends.
  • 甲至癸 - A way of numbering or categorizing using the Chinese zodiac or the ten Heavenly Stems, here likely referring to the first twenty volumes of the collection.
  • 支甲至支癸 - Likely a subdivision of the main collection, possibly indicating a secondary or supplementary section.
  • 三甲至三癸 - Another subdivision, possibly a third tier of the collection.
  • 四甲四乙 - The fourth tier, with ten volumes each.
  • 總四百二十卷 - A total of four hundred and twenty volumes, emphasizing the vast size of the collection.
  • 見陳振孫書錄解題 - Refers to the bibliographic record by Chen Zhensun, indicating the source of the information about the collection.
  • 祗據王景文夷堅別志序 - Refers to the preface by Wang Jingwen to a different version of the *Eji Zhi* (夷堅志), suggesting that Hu Yinglin's knowledge was limited to this preface.
  • 四庫所收支甲至支戊五十卷 - Refers to the volumes of the *Eji Zhi* included in the *Siku Quanshu* (四库全书), a massive compilation of Chinese literature.
  • 明仿宋刊 - Refers to a Ming dynasty edition that imitates a Song dynasty original.
  • 影寫 - Means 'photocopying' or 'reproducing' the original text.
  • 進呈 - Refers to the act of presenting a manuscript to the emperor or a high-ranking official.
  • 吾郡 - Refers to the author's hometown or region.
  • 中有玉兰堂印 - Indicates that the text has a seal or stamp from the Yulan Hall, suggesting it was once owned by someone associated with this hall.
  • 衡山文氏舊藏也 - Refers to the collection of the Wen family from Hengshan, indicating a previous owner.
  • 列子曰 - Refers to a quote from *Liezi*, a classical text attributed to Lie Yukou, a philosopher from the Warring States period.
  • 大禹行而見之 - Refers to the legendary figure Yu the Great, who is said to have seen something.
  • 伯益知而名之 - Refers to Bo Yi, a legendary figure who is said to have named the thing Yu saw.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 陳振孫 - A Song dynasty bibliographer and scholar.
  • 胡應麟 - A Ming dynasty scholar and literary critic.
  • 王景文 - A scholar who wrote a preface to a different version of the *Eji Zhi*.
  • 阮文達 - A Qing dynasty scholar and official, known for his efforts in preserving and cataloging ancient texts.
  • 宋刻 - Refers to a Song dynasty edition, indicating the high value and historical significance of the text.
  • 影寫 - The process of reproducing ancient texts, often used to preserve them.
  • 進呈 - The act of presenting a manuscript to the emperor or a high-ranking official, often for approval or preservation.
  • 吾郡 - The author's hometown or region, indicating a personal connection to the text.
  • 嚴久能 - A collector or scholar who owned the text.
  • 吳門 - Refers to Suzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province, known for its cultural and literary heritage.
  • 黃荛圃 - A collector or scholar who owned the text.
  • 汪阆原 - A collector or scholar who owned the text.
  • 胡心耘 - A collector or scholar who owned the text.
  • 玉兰堂 - A hall or collection associated with a specific owner or family.
  • 衡山文氏 - The Wen family from Hengshan, indicating a previous owner.
  • 列子 - A classical text attributed to Lie Yukou, a philosopher from the Warring States period.
  • 大禹 - Yu the Great, a legendary figure in Chinese history known for his efforts to control the floods.
  • 伯益 - Bo Yi, a legendary figure who is said to have named the thing Yu saw.

Proper Nouns

  • 夷堅志 - The title of the collection of stories.
  • 陳振孫 - A Song dynasty bibliographer and scholar.
  • 胡應麟 - A Ming dynasty scholar and literary critic.
  • 王景文 - A scholar who wrote a preface to a different version of the *Eji Zhi*.
  • 阮文達 - A Qing dynasty scholar and official.
  • 宋刻 - A Song dynasty edition.
  • 影寫 - The process of reproducing ancient texts.
  • 進呈 - The act of presenting a manuscript to the emperor or a high-ranking official.
  • 吾郡 - The author's hometown or region.
  • 嚴久能 - A collector or scholar.
  • 吳門 - Refers to Suzhou.
  • 黃荛圃 - A collector or scholar.
  • 汪阆原 - A collector or scholar.
  • 胡心耘 - A collector or scholar.
  • 玉兰堂 - A hall or collection.
  • 衡山文氏 - The Wen family from Hengshan.
  • 列子 - A classical text.
  • 大禹 - Yu the Great.
  • 伯益 - Bo Yi.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夷坚闻而志之 - 夷坚 heard it and recorded it.
  • 自来志怪之书 - Since ancient times, books about strange things.
  • 率多荒唐 - Most are largely absurd or fantastic.
  • 沿其流者 - Those who follow this tradition.
  • 最行于时 - Most popular in their time.
  • 颇与传记相似 - Quite similar to biographies.
  • 饰说剽窃 - Fabricated stories and plagiarized content.
  • 借为谈助 - Used for conversation.
  • 支甲序已自言之 - Zhi Jia's preface has already mentioned this.
  • 实非后人所及 - Indeed, it surpasses what later generations can achieve.
  • 自甲志至四甲凡三十一序 - From *Jia Zhi* to *Si Jia*, there are thirty-one prefaces in total.
  • 寓书怂恿梓行 - Wrote a letter encouraging the publication.
  • 光绪五年岁在屠维单阏阳月 - In the fifth year of Guangxu, the month of *Tuwei Danyue Yangyue*.
  • 归安陆心源撰 - Compiled by Lu Xin Yuan of Guian.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 夷坚 - A historical figure or a name given to the compiler of the text.
  • 王嘉之拾遗 - Wang Jia's *Shiyi* (拾遗), a collection of miscellaneous notes.
  • 干宝之搜神 - Gan Bao's *Soushenji* (搜神记), a famous collection of strange stories.
  • 敬叔之异苑 - Jing Shu's *Yiyan* (异苑), another collection of strange tales.
  • 徐铉之稽神 - Xu Xuan's *Jishenji* (稽神记), a book about supernatural events.
  • 成式之杂俎 - Cheng Shi's *Zazu* (杂俎), a miscellaneous collection.
  • 赵与时宾退录 - Zhao Shi's *Bin Tui Lu* (宾退录), a collection of notes and observations.
  • 光绪五年 - The fifth year of the Guangxu reign (1879).
  • 屠维单阏阳月 - A lunar calendar term referring to a specific month.
  • 归安 - A place name, likely referring to a region in China.
  • 陆心源 - The compiler of the text, Lu Xin Yuan.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence is straightforward but requires careful translation to maintain the flow.
  • This sentence establishes a comparison between *Shanhaijing* and other strange tale collections.
  • The phrase 'those who follow this tradition' needs to be clearly translated to maintain the logical flow.
  • The phrase 'most popular in their time' should be translated to convey the historical context.
  • The sentence is elliptical and requires expansion to make it clear in English.
  • The phrase 'quite similar to biographies' should be translated to maintain the contrast.
  • The phrase 'fabricated stories and plagiarized content' should be translated to convey the criticism.
  • The phrase 'used for conversation' should be translated to maintain the purpose.
  • The phrase 'Zhi Jia's preface has already mentioned this' should be translated to maintain the reference.
  • The phrase 'profound and inexhaustible' should be translated to maintain the quality of the writing.
  • The phrase 'indeed, it surpasses what later generations can achieve' should be translated to maintain the praise.
  • The phrase 'from *Jia Zhi* to *Si Jia*, there are thirty-one prefaces in total' should be translated to maintain the count.
  • The phrase 'each one is original and does not repeat the others' should be translated to maintain the originality.
  • The phrase 'Zhao Shi's *Bin Tui Lu* excerpted parts of the text and highly praised it' should be translated to maintain the praise.
  • The phrase 'indeed, it is the pinnacle of literary achievement and the ocean of short stories' should be translated to maintain the significance.
  • The phrase 'Qin Xi Hongjun, who diligently searched for and carved his ancestors' lost books' should be translated to maintain the effort.
  • The phrase 'wrote a letter encouraging the publication' should be translated to maintain the encouragement.
  • The phrase 'so he entrusted the work to the printer to fulfill Hongjun's wish' should be translated to maintain the fulfillment.
  • The phrase 'in the fifth year of Guangxu, the month of *Tuwei Danyue Yangyue*' should be translated.
  • The phrase 'Compiled by Lu Xin Yuan of Guian' should be translated.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夷堅甲志 - A collection of strange tales or anecdotes, often categorized by volume (甲, 乙, etc.). '夷堅' is the name of the collection, and '甲志' indicates the first volume.
  • 孫九鼎 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical or fictional figure. The name itself does not require translation but should be consistently rendered as 'Sun Jiuding' in English.
  • 字國鎮 - A courtesy name, which is a secondary name given to a person in addition to their given name. '國鎮' (Guozhen) is the courtesy name of Sun Jiuding.
  • 忻州人 - '忻州' is a place name, referring to Xinzhou, a city in Shanxi Province. '人' means 'person,' so '忻州人' translates to 'a person from Xinzhou.'
  • 政和癸巳 - A combination of the era name '政和' (Zhenghe) and the stem-branch calendar year '癸巳' (Gui Si). '政和' was the era name of Emperor Huizong of Song (1111–1118), and '癸巳' corresponds to 1123 CE in the Gregorian calendar.
  • 太學 - Refers to the Imperial Academy, the highest institution of learning in ancient China.
  • 七夕 - The seventh day of the seventh lunar month, known as the Qixi Festival or the Chinese Valentine's Day.
  • 竹栅巷 - '竹栅' (zhugeng) means 'bamboo fence,' and '巷' (xiang) means 'alley.' Together, '竹栅巷' refers to a street or alley named after a bamboo fence.
  • 沿汴北岸而行 - '沿' means 'along,' '汴' refers to the Bian River (a major river in ancient China), and '北岸' means 'northern bank.' This phrase describes traveling along the northern bank of the Bian River.
  • 金紫人 - '金紫' refers to high-ranking officials who wore golden and purple robes, symbolizing their status. '人' means 'person,' so '金紫人' refers to a high-ranking official.
  • 呼之於稠人中 - '呼之' means 'called out to him,' '稠人' means 'a crowd of people,' and '中' means 'in the midst.' This phrase describes calling out to Sun Jiuding in a crowded place.
  • 遽下馬 - '遽' means 'quickly' or 'hastily,' and '下馬' means 'dismounted.' This phrase describes the official quickly dismounting from his horse.
  • 國鎮久別安樂 - '國鎮' is the courtesy name of Sun Jiuding, and '久別安樂' means 'long time since we last met, and you must be well.' This is a polite greeting.
  • 細視之 - '細視' means 'looked closely at,' and '之' is a pronoun referring to the official. This phrase describes Sun Jiuding looking closely at the official.
  • 乃姊夫張兟也 - '乃' means 'it is,' '姊夫' means 'brother-in-law,' and '張兟' is the proper name of the official. This phrase identifies the official as Sun Jiuding's brother-in-law, Zhang Qing.
  • 指街北一酒肆 - '指' means 'pointed to,' '街北' means 'north of the street,' and '酒肆' means 'wine shop.' This phrase describes Zhang Qing pointing to a wine shop north of the street.
  • 可見邀于此少从容 - '可見' means 'can see,' '邀' means 'invite,' '于此' means 'here,' '少' means 'a little,' and '从容' means 'leisurely.' This phrase suggests inviting Sun Jiuding to the wine shop for a brief, leisurely visit.
  • 公富人也 - '公' is a respectful way to address someone, and '富人' means 'wealthy person.' This phrase addresses Zhang Qing as a wealthy person.
  • 岂可令穷措大买酒 - '岂可' means 'how can,' '令' means 'make,' '穷措大' refers to a poor scholar, and '买酒' means 'buy wine.' This phrase expresses the idea that it would be inappropriate for a poor scholar to buy wine for a wealthy person.
  • 我钱不中使 - '我' means 'I,' '钱' means 'money,' '不中' means 'not suitable,' and '使' means 'to use.' This phrase means 'My money is not suitable for use,' implying that Zhang Qing has no money.
  • 饮自如 - '饮' means 'drink,' '' is a variant of '酒' (wine), and '自如' means 'at ease.' This phrase describes Zhang Qing drinking wine at ease.
  • 少顷 - A time expression meaning 'a short while later.'
  • 公死已久矣 - '公' is a respectful way to address someone, '死' means 'died,' '已' means 'already,' and '久矣' means 'long ago.' This phrase means 'You have been dead for a long time.'
  • 何为在此 - '何为' means 'why,' '在此' means 'here.' This phrase asks 'Why are you here?'
  • 不然 - A negation meaning 'no' or 'not so.'
  • 君福甚壮 - '君' is a respectful way to address someone, '福' means 'fortune,' and '甚壮' means 'very strong.' This phrase means 'Your fortune is very strong.'
  • 乃说死时及孙送葬之事 - '乃' means 'then,' '说' means 'tell,' '死时' means 'time of death,' '及' means 'and,' '孙' means 'Sun' (referring to Sun Jiuding), '送葬' means 'funeral,' and '之事' means 'the matter.' This phrase describes Zhang Qing telling Sun Jiuding about his death and the funeral.
  • 无不知者 - '无' means 'none,' '知' means 'know,' and '者' is a particle. This phrase means 'there is nothing he does not know.'
  • 去年中秋我过家 - '去年' means 'last year,' '中秋' means 'Mid-Autumn Festival,' '我' means 'I,' '过' means 'visit,' and '家' means 'home.' This phrase describes Zhang Qing visiting his home last Mid-Autumn Festival.
  • 令姊辈饮酒自若 - '令姊' means 'your sister' (referring to Sun Jiuding's sister), '辈' means 'and others,' '饮酒' means 'drinking wine,' and '自若' means 'as usual.' This phrase describes Sun Jiuding's sister and others drinking wine as usual.
  • 并不相顾 - '并' means 'also,' '不' means 'not,' '相顾' means 'look at each other.' This phrase means 'they did not look at each other.'
  • 我愤恨倾酒壶击小女以出 - '我' means 'I,' '愤恨' means 'angry and resentful,' '倾酒壶' means 'pouring out the wine jug,' '击' means 'hit,' '小女' means 'young daughter,' and '以出' means 'and left.' This phrase describes Zhang Qing pouring out the wine jug and hitting his young daughter before leaving.
  • 公今在何地 - '公' is a respectful way to address someone, '今' means 'now,' '在' means 'in,' and '何地' means 'where.' This phrase asks 'Where are you now?'
  • 见为皇城司注禄判官 - '见' means 'now,' '为' means 'to be,' '皇城司' refers to the Imperial City Office, '注禄' means 'registering salaries,' and '判官' means 'judge or official.' This phrase means 'You are now the Registering Salaries Judge of the Imperial City Office.'
  • 未也 - A negation meaning 'not yet' or 'no.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 夷堅甲志 - A collection of strange tales or anecdotes, often categorized by volume (甲, 乙, etc.). '夷堅' is the name of the collection, and '甲志' indicates the first volume.
  • 七夕 - The seventh day of the seventh lunar month, known as the Qixi Festival or the Chinese Valentine's Day.
  • 太學 - The Imperial Academy, the highest institution of learning in ancient China.
  • 竹栅巷 - A street or alley named after a bamboo fence.
  • 金紫人 - High-ranking officials who wore golden and purple robes, symbolizing their status.
  • 皇城司注禄判官 - The Registering Salaries Judge of the Imperial City Office.

Proper Nouns

  • 孫九鼎 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical or fictional figure. The name itself does not require translation but should be consistently rendered as 'Sun Jiuding' in English.
  • 字國鎮 - A courtesy name, which is a secondary name given to a person in addition to their given name. '國鎮' (Guozhen) is the courtesy name of Sun Jiuding.
  • 忻州 - A place name, referring to Xinzhou, a city in Shanxi Province.
  • 政和 - An era name, referring to the Zhenghe period of the Southern Song dynasty (1111–1118).
  • 太學 - The Imperial Academy, the highest institution of learning in ancient China.
  • 竹栅 - A bamboo fence.
  • 汴 - A major river in ancient China, specifically the Bian River.
  • 北岸 - The northern bank of a river.
  • 金紫 - Refers to high-ranking officials who wore golden and purple robes, symbolizing their status.
  • 張兟 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical or fictional figure. The name itself does not require translation but should be consistently rendered as 'Zhang Qing' in English.
  • 皇城司 - The Imperial City Office.
  • 注禄 - Registering salaries.
  • 判官 - A judge or official.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 此事每十年一下 - Literal meaning is 'this matter occurs every ten years.' Likely a metaphorical expression referring to a recurring event or phenomenon.
  • 尚未见姓名 - Literal meaning is 'the names are not yet known.' Indicates that the individuals involved have not been identified.
  • 多在三十岁以后 - Literal meaning is 'mostly after the age of thirty.' Indicates a common age range for the phenomenon.
  • 官职亦不卑下 - Literal meaning is 'their official positions are not lowly.' Suggests that the individuals hold respectable or high-ranking positions.
  • 犯妇人者无月无之 - Literal meaning is 'they commit offenses against women every month.' A harsh statement, possibly referring to frequent misconduct.
  • 此吾之迹也 - Literal meaning is 'this is my trace' or 'this is my mark.' Could be a metaphorical statement about leaving a mark or being noticed.
  • 凡事当察其心 - Literal meaning is 'in all matters, one should examine the heart.' Emphasizes the importance of understanding intentions or motivations.
  • 苟心不昧 - Literal meaning is 'if the heart is not darkened' or 'if the heart is not corrupted.' Suggests that if one's intentions are pure, they can achieve anything.
  • 交直矣 - Literal meaning is 'the road is straight' or 'the path is clear.' Could be a metaphor for a situation becoming straightforward or obvious.
  • 切不得回顾 - Literal meaning is 'must not look back.' A strong warning, possibly referring to a taboo or a rule that must be strictly followed.
  • 顾即死矣 - Literal meaning is 'if you look back, you will die.' Reinforces the seriousness of the warning.
  • 公今已为阴气所侵 - Literal meaning is 'you are now being invaded by yin qi' or 'you are now being affected by negative energy.' Could be a metaphorical or spiritual warning.
  • 来日当暴下 - Literal meaning is 'you will have a severe diarrhea the next day.' Suggests a physical consequence.
  • 宜毋吃他药 - Literal meaning is 'you should not take other medicines.' Advises against additional treatments.
  • 服平胃散足矣 - Literal meaning is 'taking Píng Wèi San is sufficient.' Recommends a specific remedy.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 淫祠 - Literal meaning 'excessive shrines' or 'idolatrous temples,' referring to unauthorized or excessive religious establishments.
  • 屏三百区 - Means 'destroyed three hundred shrines,' indicating the scale of the campaign against idolatrous temples.
  • 三五扣神扃 - A poetic way of saying 'knocking on the divine door,' symbolizing communication with the spiritual realm.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蒋静叔明 - The name of the magistrate, who was from Yixing and served as the magistrate of Anren in Raoyang.
  • 宜兴 - A place name, likely referring to Yixing, a city in Jiangsu Province.
  • 饶州安仁 - Refers to Anren, a county in Raoyang, which was part of the Jiangxi Province.
  • 柳将军庙 - The temple dedicated to the deity known as 'Willow General,' which was the only one spared from destruction due to its perceived sanctity.
  • 杉一株 - A single pine tree, which is significant in the story as it is the object of the magistrate's intended destruction.
  • 中书舍人 - A high-ranking official position in the imperial bureaucracy, responsible for drafting important documents.
  • 寿春江宁 - Refers to Shouzhou and Jiangning, both important cities in Jiangsu Province.
  • 江东安仁 - Refers to the region of Jiangdong, Anren, where the magistrate later served.
  • 大司成 - A high-ranking official position, often associated with the head of the Imperial Academy.
  • 显谟阁直学士 - A title indicating a scholar-official in the Xianmo Hall, responsible for academic and advisory roles.

Proper Nouns

  • 蒋静叔明 - The name of the magistrate.
  • 宜兴 - A place name, likely referring to Yixing, a city in Jiangsu Province.
  • 饶州安仁 - Refers to Anren, a county in Raoyang, which was part of the Jiangxi Province.
  • 柳将军庙 - The temple dedicated to the deity known as 'Willow General.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 金國天會十四年四月 - The 14th year of the Tianhui era of the Jin dynasty, April. The Jin dynasty (1115–1234) was a powerful northern Chinese state that ruled over northern China after the fall of the Northern Song. 天會 (Tianhui) was the era name of the Jurchen emperor Wanyan Jing, who ruled from 1123 to 1135. The text is likely a historical or legendary account from the Jin period.
  • 中京 - Central Capital. In the context of the Jin dynasty, 中京 may refer to the central capital, possibly Zhongdu or another administrative center, though the exact location is uncertain.
  • 土河 - Earth river. This may be a poetic or metaphorical term for a dry riverbed or a natural stream, or it may refer to a specific location.
  • 丐者 - Beggar. In classical Chinese, this term often carries a connotation of poverty, social marginalization, or spiritual wandering.
  • 病癫 - Afflicted with madness. In traditional Chinese medicine, this could refer to a mental illness, possibly with spiritual or supernatural causes.
  • 石氏 - The Shi family. This is a common Chinese surname, and the narrative follows a member of this family.
  • 開茶肆 - Opened a tea shop. Tea shops were common in urban areas and often run by families, especially in the Song and Jin periods.
  • 令幼女行茶 - Sent her young daughter to serve tea. This suggests a family-run business where the daughter was involved in daily operations.
  • 呂翁 - Master Lu. This is likely a Daoist or alchemist figure, possibly a spiritual teacher or a practitioner of longevity arts.

  • 至有數四者 - 'Up to four or five times,' indicating the frequency of the transaction.

  • 往柁樓便旋 - 'He went to the stern of the boat to relieve himself.' '柁樓' refers to the stern or steering area of a boat, and '便旋' is a euphemism for going to the bathroom or relieving oneself.
  • 為回風所擊 - 'Was struck by a sudden wind,' indicating a sudden and unexpected event.

  • 方號呼次 - 'While he was shouting for help,' showing the ongoing action.
  • 冉冉立水上 - 'Stood slowly on the water,' indicating a ghostly or supernatural appearance.
  • 隨風赴舟所 - 'Followed the wind to the boat’s location,' indicating that the ghost was guided by the wind.

  • 急取之 - 'They quickly took him,' showing the urgency of the rescue.

  • 人以為積善報云 - 'People believed it was a reward for his accumulated virtue.' This is a common expression in classical Chinese literature, indicating that the person was rewarded for his good deeds.

  • 員外 - A title given to wealthy gentry or officials who had the financial means to support the state. It was a common honorific for men of means and moral character.
  • 子侄 - Refers to nephews, younger brothers, or younger relatives, indicating a family context.

  • 柁樓 - A specific part of a boat, usually the stern or steering area. This term reflects the detailed nautical terminology used in classical Chinese texts.
  • 回風 - 'Sudden wind' or 'back wind' can be interpreted as a strong gust or a supernatural wind, often used to describe dramatic or supernatural events.
  • 積善報 - 'Reward for accumulated virtue' is a common moral concept in Confucian and Daoist thought, suggesting that good deeds are rewarded in this life or the next.
  • 李郁光祖 - The name of the storyteller or recorder of the tale. It is not a well-known historical figure, but the name is preserved as is, since it is a proper noun.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 泉州僧本偁说其表兄为海贾，欲往三佛齐，法当南行三日而东，否则值焦上，舩必糜碎。此人行时偶风迅，舩驶既二日半，意其当转而东，即回柁，然已无及，遂落焦上，一舟尽溺。此人独得一木，浮水三日，漂至一岛畔，度其必死，舍木登岸，行数十步，得小迳，路甚光洁，若常有人行者。久之，有妇人至，举体无片缕，言语啁口扎，见外人甚喜，携手归石室中，至夜与共寝。天明举大石窒其外，妇人独出，至日晡时归，必赍异果至，其味珍甚，皆世所无者。留稍久，始听自便，如是七八年，生三子。一日纵步至海际，适有舟抵岸，亦泉人以风误至者，乃旧相识，急登之。时妇人继来，度不可及，呼其人骂之，极口悲啼，扑地气几绝。其人从蓬底举手谢之，亦为掩涕。此舟已张帆，乃得归。

  • 震死 - Literally means 'killed by thunder.' In this context, it refers to being struck by lightning, often interpreted as divine punishment.
  • 挈尸至云外 - Literally means 'carried the body to the clouds.' This is a metaphorical expression indicating that the body was lifted high into the sky, possibly by divine intervention.
  • 朱衣人 - Literally means 'man in red clothing.' In Chinese folklore, a man in red is often associated with deities or supernatural beings.
  • 扑于平地 - Literally means 'fell to the ground.' This describes the physical act of falling to the ground after being lifted.
  • 项上并胁下 - Refers to the neck and sides of the body.
  • 斧迹 - Literally means 'axe marks.' This indicates that the body had marks resembling those made by an axe.
  • 出青黑汁数升 - Literally means 'spilled several liters of green-black fluid.' This suggests that the body exuded a dark, possibly blood-like substance.
  • 同村港西亦有段二十六者 - Refers to another person named Duan Erliu who lived in the same village.
  • 此人元储谷二仓 - Literally means 'this person originally stored grain in two granaries.' This indicates that the person had stored a large amount of grain.
  • 岁饥闭不肯出 - Literally means 'refused to sell during the year of famine.' This describes the person's refusal to release grain during a time of scarcity.
  • 天诛之 - Literally means 'killed by heaven.' This implies divine retribution.
  • 谷皆为火焚 - Literally means 'the grain was all burned by fire.' This indicates that the stored grain was destroyed by fire.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '夜有虎自东山逾破城，入其园，伤圈豕而去' describes a series of events: a tiger breaks through a city wall, enters the garden, injures pigs, and leaves. The sentence is structured as a sequence of actions, but the exact timing and order of events could be ambiguous.
  • The phrase '举室虑其复至' is somewhat ambiguous in terms of who is doing the fearing. It could be interpreted as the entire household fearing the tiger's return, or it could be interpreted as the household collectively fearing something else.
  • The sentence '太尉不以为异，且高吟曰' is a bit elliptical. It could be interpreted as the Grand Marshal not considering the tiger's appearance unusual, and then reciting a poem, or it could be interpreted as the Grand Marshal not considering the tiger's appearance unusual and then reciting a poem.
  • The phrase '叔母戏续其下云' is somewhat ambiguous in terms of who is continuing the poem. It could be interpreted as the aunt continuing the poem, or it could be interpreted as the aunt continuing the poem after the Grand Marshal.
  • The phrase '邻里传以为笑' is somewhat ambiguous in terms of what is being passed around as a joke. It could be interpreted as the neighbors passing around the poem, or it could be interpreted as the neighbors passing around the story of the tiger and the poem.
  • The phrase '魁天下士' is somewhat ambiguous in terms of whether it refers to the Grand Marshal becoming the top scholar, or whether it refers to the Grand Marshal's son becoming the top scholar.
  • The phrase '登政府' is somewhat ambiguous in terms of whether it refers to the Grand Marshal entering the government, or whether it refers to the Grand Marshal's son entering the government.
  • The phrase '恩得官至大夫' is somewhat ambiguous in terms of whether it refers to the Grand Marshal receiving an official position, or whether it refers to the Grand Marshal's son receiving an official position.
  • The phrase '所谓颠狗低蹲之语，乃其母诗实先谶也' is somewhat ambiguous in terms of whether it refers to the words 'mad dog, stay low' as a prophecy, or whether it refers to the words 'mad dog, stay low' as a poem that had foreseen the future.

  • 或诉其过于朝 - 或诉 means someone reported, and 过于朝 means to the court. This phrase indicates that someone reported Vang Liang's misdeeds to the imperial court.
  • 直龙图阁 - 直龙图阁 is an official title, indicating a high-ranking government position.
  • 俾鞫之 - 俾 means to order, and 鞫之 means to investigate. This phrase indicates that the court ordered an investigation into Vang Liang's affairs.

  • 比断敕至 - 比断 means by the time the verdict was issued, and 敕至 means the imperial edict arrived. This phrase indicates that the verdict came after Vang Liang had already died.
  • 权厝于英之南山寺 - 权厝 means temporarily interred, and 英之南山寺 refers to the Nan Shan Temple in Yingzhou. This phrase describes where Vang Liang's body was temporarily buried.
  • 追录宝货甚多 - 追录 means to seize and record, and 宝货甚多 means a large amount of treasure. This phrase indicates that a significant amount of wealth was seized from Vang Liang.
  • 摄帅事 - 摄 means to temporarily assume, and 帅事 refers to military or administrative duties. This phrase indicates that Chen Shu temporarily took over administrative duties.
  • 代还 - 代还 means to return in place of someone else. This phrase indicates that Chen Shu returned to his original position.
  • 以它事复为转运使许君所劾 - 以它事 means for other matters, and 复为转运使许君所劾 means was again impeached by the governor Xu. This phrase indicates that Chen Shu was impeached for other reasons.
  • 下廷尉 - 下廷尉 means to be sent to the court of justice. This phrase indicates that Chen Shu was sent to the court of justice.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 郑良、字少张。英州人。 - Vang Liang, also known as Shao Zhang, was from Yingzhou (modern-day Guangdong).
  • 宣和中。仕至右文殿修撰广南东西路转运使。 - During the Xuanhe period (1119-1125), he held the position of Right Literary Editor and Transport Commissioner for the Guangdong and Guangxi regions.
  • 靖康元年。或诉其过于朝。 - In the first year of the Jingkang era (1126), someone reported his misdeeds to the court.
  • 朝廷遣直龙图阁陈述为漕。俾鞫之。 - The court dispatched Chen Shu, who held the title of Directing the Dragon Pavilion, to investigate Vang Liang's affairs.
  • 述至英。良居家。初不知其故。盛具延述。 - Chen Shu arrived in Yingzhou, and Vang Liang, unaware of the reason, warmly invited him.
  • 述亦推心与饮。缔同官之好。 - Chen Shu also treated him kindly and they drank together, forming a friendly relationship.
  • 至广州。始遣使逮良下狱。穷治其赃。 - Upon arriving in Guangzhou, Chen Shu sent someone to arrest Vang Liang and investigate his corruption.
  • 榜笞不可计。奏案上。方得出狱。出之一日而良死。 - Vang Liang was severely punished, and the case was reported to the court. He was released from prison but died the next day.
  • 比断敕至。止于停官编隶。已无及矣。 - By the time the verdict was issued, Vang Liang had already died, and he was only demoted and exiled.
  • 家人未能葬。权厝于英之南山寺。 - Vang Liang's family could not bury him immediately, so he was temporarily interred at the Nan Shan Temple in Yingzhou.
  • 所追录宝货甚多。 - A large amount of treasure was seized from Vang Liang.
  • 述遂摄帅事。建炎二年代还。 - Chen Shu then temporarily assumed administrative duties and returned to his position in the second year of the Jianyan era.
  • 以它事复为转运使许君所劾。下廷尉。削籍编置英州。 - Chen Shu was again impeached by Governor Xu for other matters and was sent to the court of justice, where he was removed from the official register and exiled to Yingzhou.
  • 太守置之南山。时良已迁葬数日。殡宫空。欲述居之。或告以实。 - The prefect placed Chen Shu in the Nan Shan Temple. By then, Vang Liang had been reburied, and the temple was empty. The monks told Chen Shu the truth.
  • 述曰、吾前治其狱。王事也。今已死。何足畏。即居之。才三四日。白昼见良。惊曰。郑良何敢来。即感疾死。 - Chen Shu said, 'I investigated his case for the sake of the state. He is already dead, what have I to fear?' He stayed in the temple, but after three or four days, he saw Vang Liang in broad daylight, shocked and said, 'How dare you come here?' and immediately fell ill and died.
  • 时建炎二年也。良之宅今三分为天庆观州学驿舍。其家徙江西云。三事英僧希赐言。 - This occurred in the second year of the Jianyan era. Vang Liang's mansion is now divided into the Tianqing Temple, the prefectural school, and the postal station. His family moved to Jiangxi. The monks of Yingzhou provided commentary on the three matters.

  • 日诵圆觉经。 - Recites the *Yuanjue Jing* (Avatamsaka Sutra) every day.
  • 释服不辍。 - Does not cease from the *shifú* (the mourning period).
  • 要知真性。本圆本觉。不觉不圆。是为凡夫。 - Philosophical statement: 'To know the true nature: it is originally perfect and enlightened. If one is not enlightened or not perfect, that is the state of an ordinary person.'
  • 我不诵经。要遮眼耳。 - I do not recite sutras to cover my eyes and ears. Metaphorical expression meaning 'I do not recite sutras just to distract myself.'
  • 长子楹。登进士第。 - 'Her eldest son, Ying, passed the imperial examination as a *jinshi*.' Standard way of saying someone passed the *jinshi* examination.
  • 为贺州签判。迎母至官。 - 'Served as the *qianpan* (deputy official) of Hezhou, and brought his mother to the official residence.' *签判* was an official position in the Song Dynasty.
  • 沐浴易衣。明日食罢。盥漱如常。 - 'Bathed and changed clothes. The next day, after eating, washed and brushed teeth as usual.' A sequence of actions indicating normal condition before death.
  • 忽收足端坐。两中指结印。瞑目而逝。 - 'Suddenly raised her feet and sat upright. Her two middle fingers formed a mudra. She closed her eyes and passed away.' *结印* is a Buddhist or Daoist gesture (mudra).
  • 家人仓黄召医。已无及矣。 - 'The family rushed to call a physician, but it was too late.' *仓黄* is a classical term for 'in a hurry' or 'in panic.'
  • 郡守范直清帅其属瞻礼。叹曰。大丈夫不能如此。 - 'The county governor, Fan Zhiqing, led his subordinates to pay respects. He said, 'A great man cannot be like this.' *帅其属* is 'led his subordinates.' *瞻礼* is to pay respects.
  • 命画工写其像。像成。惟目睛未点。 - 'Ordered the painter to draw her image. When the image was completed, the eyes were not yet painted.' Common motif in Chinese religious or miraculous stories.
  • 乃祷曰。精神全在阿堵中。愿赐开示。 - 'He prayed, 'Her spirit is entirely in this.' May you grant us an opening (to see).' *阿堵* is a classical term for 'this' or 'that.'
  • 俄两目炯然。子孙扶视。皆谓再生。点睛讫复瞑。 - 'Soon, her eyes gleamed brightly. Her descendants supported her and looked at her, all saying she was reborn. After the eyes were painted, she closed her eyes again.' *炯然* is 'shining brightly.'
  • 时年七十七。傅雩彦济言。 - 'She was 77 years old. Fu Yu Yanji said.' *傅雩彦济* is the name of the compiler or commentator.
  • 昌国商人 - 'A merchant from Changguo.' *昌国* is likely a place name or title; best left as *Changguo* unless further evidence is found.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贺氏 - A surname, likely referring to a woman from the *He* family. No specific historical figure is known by this name.
  • 吉州水新人 - *Jizhou Shuixin* – A location in Jiangxi province. *Shuixin* may be a district or village within Jizhou.
  • 圆觉经 - *Avatamsaka Sutra* or *Sutra of Perfect Enlightenment*. Major Mahayana Buddhist text.
  • 释服不辍 - *Shifú* refers to the mourning rites. Buddhist practice of continuing the mourning period.
  • 登进士第 - Standard reference to passing the *jinshi* examination in the imperial civil service system.
  • 贺州签判 - *Hezhou qianpan* – A local official position in the Song Dynasty.
  • 郡守范直清 - *County governor Fan Zhiqing* – A local official in the Song Dynasty.
  • 傅雩彦济言 - *Fu Yu Yanji* – The name of the compiler or commentator. No historical figure is known by this name.
  • 昌国商人 - *A merchant from Changguo* – *Changguo* may be a misspelling of *Changguo* (a place), or it could be a title.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The title '佛还钗' may be a metaphorical or symbolic expression rather than a literal description.
  • The sequence '脱身贼手，出郭于水中行' describes a perilous escape, but the exact nature of the danger and the setting may be ambiguous.
  • The actions of chanting and discarding the hairpin in '诵观音佛名，首插金钗恐为累，掷置水中' may be linked to a spiritual or practical decision.
  • The encounter with the mysterious figure in '迷所向，有白衣老媪在岸，呼之令上，指示其路' is a key event, but the nature of her guidance may be open to interpretation.
  • The dream and its aftermath in '梦狸猫自项背入腹中，从此日甚' are described in a way that may suggest a supernatural cause for the illness.
  • The vow and daily practice in '发意诵观音菩萨百万声，日持大悲咒百八遍' are described in a way that emphasizes the intensity of the spiritual effort.
  • The dream sequence in '青衣童笼一鸡，前猫自僧口出，径入笼擒鸡' is symbolic, but the exact meaning of the actions may be ambiguous.

Proper Nouns

  • 观音菩萨 - Avalokiteshvara, the Bodhisattva of compassion.
  • 大悲咒 - The Great Compassion Mantra.
  • 荐福 - A temple or place of worship, possibly specific to the story.
  • 翻胃 - A medical condition, not a proper noun but a term that may require contextual explanation.

  • 福州候官县 - Refers to the location where the story takes place, specifically the county of Houguan in Fuzhou.
  • 官府 - Refers to the government office or court where the girl was taken.
  • 三人鼎足而坐 - Describes a scene where three people are sitting in a triangular formation, symbolizing a balanced and formal setting.
  • 郑彦和知刚说 - The name of the narrator or source of the story, suggesting that the story is based on a known account or tradition.

  • The confirmation of the mistake in '所追王氏，今误矣' can be read as a formal correction, but the exact tone and context can be slightly ambiguous.
  • The question in '公何以知之' can be read as a challenge or a request for explanation, but the exact intent can be slightly ambiguous.
  • The identification in '此吾女也' can be read as a clear statement, but the exact emotional tone can be slightly ambiguous.
  • The confirmation in '果误矣' can be read as a formal statement, but the exact tone and context can be slightly ambiguous.
  • The statement in '王法无亲' can be read as a general principle, but the exact application can be slightly ambiguous.
  • The exception in '今日却有亲' can be read as a humorous or ironic comment, but the exact tone can be slightly ambiguous.
  • The laughter in '皆大笑' can be read as a sign of relief or amusement, but the exact emotion can be slightly ambiguous.
  • The release in '乃放我还' can be read as a formal action, but the exact context and implications can be slightly ambiguous.

  • 睡觉至亥时 - Means 'awoke at the hour of Hai,' referring to the time of night (亥时 corresponds to 9 PM to 11 PM in the traditional Chinese time system).
  • 闻其说 - Means 'heard this account,' concluding the narrative.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 绍兴 - The reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Song, used to date events during his rule.
  • 洪州 - Refers to Nanchang, a city in Jiangxi province, which was an important administrative center during the Southern Song.
  • 监税秉义郎 - A title for a tax collector and military officer, indicating the official position of Xie Xidan.
  • 进士 - Refers to those who passed the highest level of the imperial examination, indicating a high degree of scholarly achievement.
  • 妻族 - Refers to the family of one's spouse, indicating a personal connection.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 張六參入朝 - Formal expression meaning 'Zhang Six entered the court.' The use of '六參' (liù cān) suggests a specific ritual or ceremony, possibly a formal audience with the emperor.
  • 方傳呼 - Means 'just as the herald was calling out' or 'as the announcement was being made.' This indicates the ceremonial aspect of the scene.
  • 適有數鬼來此 - Colloquial expression meaning 'just now several ghosts came here.' The use of '適' (shì) emphasizes the immediacy of the event.
  • 一判官綠袍 - Describes a judge wearing a green robe. '綠袍' (lǜ páo) is a specific description that may carry cultural significance.
  • 滿面皆豬毛逆生 - Vivid description meaning 'with a face covered in pig bristles growing against the grain.' This is a highly unusual and metaphorical description.
  • 問我踪迹 - Means 'asked about my whereabouts' or 'inquired about my past.' This suggests a supernatural interrogation.
  • 答云、夫范友 - Direct quote meaning 'I replied, 'My husband, Fan You.' The use of '答云' (dá yún) indicates a formal or literary style of reporting dialogue.
  • 本黄河埽岸兵士 - Means 'originally a soldier guarding the Yellow River embankment.' This provides background on Fan You's past.
  • 后从至行在 - Means 'later followed him to the imperial court.' This indicates a change in Fan You's status.
  • 今为院子 - Means 'now is a servant.' This describes Fan You's current role.
  • 判官颔之 - Means 'the judge nodded in agreement.' The use of '颔之' (hàn zhī) is a literary way of describing a nod.
  • 方徘徊间 - Means 'while he was hesitating' or 'while he was lingering.' This suggests a moment of indecision.
  • 忽闻人呼右司来 - Means 'suddenly heard someone calling the Right Censor.' This indicates a sudden interruption.
  • 诸鬼皆奔散 - Means 'all the ghosts fled.' This describes the reaction of the ghosts.
  • 独判官叹恨曰 - Means 'only the judge sighed and said.' The use of '叹恨' (tàn hèn) conveys a sense of regret or frustration.
  • 收气不尽矣 - Means 'the vital energy is not fully depleted.' This is a metaphorical expression.
  • 方出门去 - Means 'just as he was leaving.' This indicates a specific moment in time.
  • 犹未远也 - Means 'he was not far away.' This provides a spatial reference.
  • 妻复起能饮食 - Means 'the wife revived and could eat and drink.' This describes a miraculous recovery.
  • 又十日竟死 - Means 'but after ten days, she died.' This indicates the final outcome.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 张渊道 - Zhang Yuandao, a historical figure who served as a Right Censor during the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 绍兴五年 - The fifth year of the Shaoxing era (1135 AD), a period during the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 右司郎官 - A high-ranking official in the Song Dynasty government, responsible for various administrative duties.
  • 黄河埽岸兵士 - Soldiers responsible for maintaining the embankments of the Yellow River, a critical task due to the frequent flooding.
  • 西川 - The western region of Sichuan, an important area in the Song Dynasty.
  • 水手 - Watermen, individuals skilled in water transportation and navigation.
  • 行在 - The temporary capital or imperial court, often used when the emperor was not in the permanent capital.
  • 院子 - A servant or attendant, often of a lower rank.
  • 判官 - A judge or official in the underworld, a common figure in Chinese folklore and literature.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 方传呼，范妻忽自棺中举手撼其夫 - This sentence is elliptical and can be read as 'just as the herald was calling out, Fan's wife suddenly raised her hand and shook her husband from the coffin.'
  • 判官颔之，方徘徊间，忽闻人呼右司来 - This sequence is compact and can be read as 'the judge nodded in agreement, while he was hesitating, suddenly heard someone calling the Right Censor.'
  • 独判官叹恨曰，收气不尽矣，方出门去，犹未远也 - This sequence is also compact and can be read as 'only the judge sighed and said, 'The vital energy is not fully depleted,' just as he was leaving, he was not far away.'

  • 范友 - Fan You, the husband.
  • 范妻 - Fan's wife.
  • 右司 - The Right Censor.
  • 判官 - The judge in the underworld.

  • 家於拱州 - Means 'resided in Gongzhou,' where '家於' is a phrase indicating residence.
  • 流民日過門 - Means 'refugees passed by the door daily,' where '日' emphasizes the frequency.

  • 大雷雨 - Means 'a heavy thunderstorm,' where '大' emphasizes the intensity.

  • 拒不应 - Means 'he refused to respond,' where '拒' indicates refusal.
  • 形状既异 - Means 'her appearance was already strange,' where '形状' refers to physical appearance and '异' means 'strange.'
